sound_type: deep resonant buzz -- wing vibration at 130-240 Hz, warm and full, body as resonating chamber, deeper than mosquito whine, buzz pollination (sonication), E=3, wildflower meadow habitat, first POLLINATOR in engine
ecosystem: Wildflower meadow. Open meadows with diverse flowering plants, alpine and subalpine meadows, garden edges, forest clearings.
season: Spring through autumn. Peak activity in midsummer when wildflower diversity peaks. Diurnal.

coordinates.description: Reference for wildflower meadow habitat in the PNW Cascades foothills, Washington. Bumblebee buzz audible in alpine/subalpine meadows.

taxonomy_note.description: NEW FAMILY: Apidae (42nd). NEW ORDER: Hymenoptera (bees, wasps, ants). Fifth insect order in engine: Orthoptera (87-88), Hemiptera (89), Diptera (91), Hymenoptera (92). First POLLINATOR. First mutualist. Buzz pollination (sonication) -- vibrating flowers at specific frequencies to release pollen. Sound performs ecological work.
taxonomy_note.infraclass_note: Insecta. Insect streak continues from degree 91.
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Apidae from Latin apis ('bee'). Hymenoptera from Greek hymen ('membrane') + pteron ('wing'), referring to the membranous wings.
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Family-level entry. Multiple Bombus species in the PNW: B. occidentalis (western bumblebee), B. mixtus (fuzzy-horned), B. melanopygus (black-tailed), others.
taxonomy_note.order_note: Hymenoptera -- NEW ORDER. Bees, wasps, ants. Fifth insect order. Most ecologically significant -- keystone pollinators.
taxonomy_note.class_note: Insecta -- continues from degree 91 (mosquito, Diptera). Insect streak at two consecutive (91-92).
taxonomy_note.family_position: Apidae is the 42nd family and the first Hymenoptera family in the engine.

song_metrics.fundamental_frequency_range_hz: 130-240 Hz (wing vibration during flight). Sonication: 130-400 Hz.
song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: NOT a song or call. WING VIBRATION (flight buzz) and SONICATION (buzz pollination). Second wing-produced sound after mosquito (91).
song_metrics.song_duration_s: Continuous during flight. Sonication bursts: 0.5-2 seconds per flower visit. Active for hours during warm days.
song_metrics.note_structure: No note structure. Continuous buzz during flight. Sonication: intensified buzz bursts during flower contact.
song_metrics.note_types: Flight buzz (continuous), sonication buzz (intensified, during flower visits), defensive buzz (higher frequency, when threatened).
song_metrics.phrase_structure: No phrase structure. Continuous buzz modulated by activity: lower during cruising flight, higher during sonication, higher still during defensive buzzing.
song_metrics.timbre: Deep. Resonant. Warm. Full-bodied. The large, furry body amplifies the wing vibration. Rich harmonics. Much warmer than mosquito's thin whine.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=3. Individual bumblebees clearly audible at several meters. Multiple bumblebees create a warm ambient hum in wildflower meadows. Sonication is louder than cruising flight. E=3 reflects the warm, present quality.
song_metrics.tempo: Wingbeat: approximately 130-240 beats per second. Continuous. Modulated by activity.
song_metrics.context: POLLINATION: the buzz is both a byproduct of flight and a functional tool for buzz pollination. Bumblebees visit flowers, and the buzz itself releases pollen. First species where sound performs ecological work.
song_metrics.regional_variation: Multiple Bombus species in PNW meadows. All produce similar deep buzz within the Apidae range. Higher elevation species may have slightly different wingbeat frequencies.
song_metrics.key_property: DEEP RESONANT BUZZ -- the 93rd acoustic element. Second wing-produced sound (after mosquito 91). Deep, warm, resonant at 130-240 Hz. Body as resonating chamber. Buzz pollination (sonication) -- first functional sound in engine. E=3. NEW FAMILY Apidae (42nd). NEW ORDER Hymenoptera. First POLLINATOR.

physical_description.overall: Family-level entry. Large, robust, densely furred bees. Body length 10-25 mm. Distinctly fuzzy appearance with black and yellow (or orange, red, white) banding patterns.
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: For degree 92: (1) LARGE SIZE -- 10-25 mm, much larger than honeybees. (2) DENSE FUR -- distinctly fuzzy, thermoregulatory. (3) BANDING -- black with yellow/orange/red/white bands. (4) ROBUST BUILD -- round, stocky body. (5) DEEP BUZZ -- audible at distance.
physical_description.fur_detail: Dense, velvety fur covering most of body. Fur is a key diagnostic feature -- bumblebees are distinctly fuzzy. Color banding varies by species: B. occidentalis has white-tailed pattern, B. melanopygus has orange dorsal band with black tail.
physical_description.field_id: Identified by SOUND (deep buzz) and APPEARANCE (large, fuzzy, banded). Active in wildflower meadows on warm days. Distinctive hovering and flower-visiting behavior. The buzz is audible before the bee is seen.

nodes.title: Deep Resonant Buzz -- 93rd Acoustic Element
nodes.description: Wing vibration at 130-240 Hz. Deep, warm, resonant. Body as resonating chamber. Richer than mosquito's thin whine. E=3. Second wing-produced sound.

nodes.title: NEW ORDER: Hymenoptera (Fifth Insect Order)
nodes.description: Bees, wasps, ants. Fifth insect order: Orthoptera, Hemiptera, Diptera, Hymenoptera. NEW FAMILY: Apidae (42nd). Most ecologically significant.

nodes.title: First POLLINATOR -- Ecological Keystone
nodes.description: First pollinator in the engine. First mutualist. Sound tied to ecological service. Wildflower meadow depends on bumblebee. Bumblebee depends on meadow.

nodes.title: Buzz Pollination (Sonication)
nodes.description: Vibrating flowers at specific frequencies (130-400 Hz) to release pollen. First functional sound -- sound performs ecological work. Not just byproduct but tool.

nodes.title: Wildflower Meadow Habitat
nodes.description: Open meadows with diverse flowering plants. Brightest habitat. Connected to marmot (75) and golden-mantled GS (79) mountain meadow habitats.

nodes.title: The Trance Mission Parallel
nodes.description: Both cross-pollinators. Trance Mission carries music between traditions. Bumblebee carries pollen between flowers. Both create through connection.

cross_references.relationship: CONSECUTIVE INSECT, WING-PRODUCED SOUND. Mosquito (Diptera, 400-600 Hz, E=2, thin whine) to bumblebee (Hymenoptera, 130-240 Hz, E=3, deep buzz). Both wing-produced but completely different character. Two wing-sounds.
INSECT STREAK. Cricket (Orthoptera, stridulation), katydid (Orthoptera), cicada (Hemiptera, tymbal), mosquito (Diptera, wing), bumblebee (Hymenoptera, wing). Five insect orders.
INSECT DIVERSITY. Cicada (Hemiptera, tymbal, consumer) and bumblebee (Hymenoptera, wing, pollinator). Different orders, mechanisms, and ecological roles.
MEADOW SHARED. Marmot in alpine/subalpine meadows (75). Bumblebee in wildflower meadows (92). Overlapping mountain meadow habitats.
MEADOW/EDGE SHARED. Ground squirrel in meadow/edge (79). Bumblebee in wildflower meadows (92). Both diurnal meadow residents.
